    17→package workqueue
    18→
    19→import (
    20→	"sync"
    21→	"time"
    22→
    23→	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/sets"
    24→	"k8s.io/utils/clock"
    25→)
    26→
    27→// Deprecated: Interface is deprecated, use TypedInterface instead.
    28→type Interface TypedInterface[any]
    29→
    30→type TypedInterface[T comparable] interface {
    31→	Add(item T)
    32→	Len() int
    33→	Get() (item T, shutdown bool)
    34→	Done(item T)
    35→	ShutDown()
    36→	ShutDownWithDrain()
    37→	ShuttingDown() bool
    38→}
    39→
    40→// Queue is the underlying storage for items. The functions below are always
    41→// called from the same goroutine.
    42→type Queue[T comparable] interface {
    43→	// Touch can be hooked when an existing item is added again. This may be
    44→	// useful if the implementation allows priority change for the given item.
    45→	Touch(item T)
    46→	// Push adds a new item.
    47→	Push(item T)
    48→	// Len tells the total number of items.
    49→	Len() int
    50→	// Pop retrieves an item.
    51→	Pop() (item T)
    52→}
    53→
    54→// DefaultQueue is a slice based FIFO queue.
    55→func DefaultQueue[T comparable]() Queue[T] {
    56→	return new(queue[T])
    57→}
    58→
    59→// queue is a slice which implements Queue.
    60→type queue[T comparable] []T
    61→
    62→func (q *queue[T]) Touch(item T) {}
    63→
    64→func (q *queue[T]) Push(item T) {
    65→	*q = append(*q, item)
    66→}
    67→
    68→func (q *queue[T]) Len() int {
    69→	return len(*q)
    70→}
    71→
    72→func (q *queue[T]) Pop() (item T) {
    73→	item = (*q)[0]
    74→
    75→	// The underlying array still exists and reference this object, so the object will not be garbage collected.
    76→	(*q)[0] = *new(T)
    77→	*q = (*q)[1:]
    78→
    79→	return item
    80→}
    81→
    82→// QueueConfig specifies optional configurations to customize an Interface.
    83→// Deprecated: use TypedQueueConfig instead.
    84→type QueueConfig = TypedQueueConfig[any]
    85→
    86→type TypedQueueConfig[T comparable] struct {
    87→	// Name for the queue. If unnamed, the metrics will not be registered.
    88→	Name string
    89→
    90→	// MetricsProvider optionally allows specifying a metrics provider to use for the queue
    91→	// instead of the global provider.
    92→	MetricsProvider MetricsProvider
    93→
    94→	// Clock ability to inject real or fake clock for testing purposes.
    95→	Clock clock.WithTicker
    96→
    97→	// Queue provides the underlying queue to use. It is optional and defaults to slice based FIFO queue.
    98→	Queue Queue[T]
    99→}
   100→
   101→// New constructs a new work queue (see the package comment).
   102→//
   103→// Deprecated: use NewTyped instead.
   104→func New() *Type {
   105→	return NewWithConfig(QueueConfig{
   106→		Name: "",
   107→	})
   108→}
   109→
   110→// NewTyped constructs a new work queue (see the package comment).
   111→func NewTyped[T comparable]() *Typed[T] {
   112→	return NewTypedWithConfig(TypedQueueConfig[T]{
   113→		Name: "",
   114→	})
   115→}
   116→
   117→// NewWithConfig constructs a new workqueue with ability to
   118→// customize different properties.
   119→//
   120→// Deprecated: use NewTypedWithConfig instead.
   121→func NewWithConfig(config QueueConfig) *Type {
   122→	return NewTypedWithConfig(config)
   123→}
   124→
   125→// NewTypedWithConfig constructs a new workqueue with ability to
   126→// customize different properties.
   127→func NewTypedWithConfig[T comparable](config TypedQueueConfig[T]) *Typed[T] {
   128→	return newQueueWithConfig(config, defaultUnfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod)
   129→}
   130→
   131→// NewNamed creates a new named queue.
   132→// Deprecated: Use NewWithConfig instead.
   133→func NewNamed(name string) *Type {
   134→	return NewWithConfig(QueueConfig{
   135→		Name: name,
   136→	})
   137→}
   138→
   139→// newQueueWithConfig constructs a new named workqueue
   140→// with the ability to customize different properties for testing purposes
   141→func newQueueWithConfig[T comparable](config TypedQueueConfig[T], updatePeriod time.Duration) *Typed[T] {
   142→	metricsProvider := globalMetricsProvider
   143→	if config.MetricsProvider != nil {
   144→		metricsProvider = config.MetricsProvider
   145→	}
   146→
   147→	if config.Clock == nil {
   148→		config.Clock = clock.RealClock{}
   149→	}
   150→
   151→	if config.Queue == nil {
   152→		config.Queue = DefaultQueue[T]()
   153→	}
   154→
   155→	return newQueue(
   156→		config.Clock,
   157→		config.Queue,
   158→		newQueueMetrics[T](metricsProvider, config.Name, config.Clock),
   159→		updatePeriod,
   160→	)
   161→}
   162→
   163→func newQueue[T comparable](c clock.WithTicker, queue Queue[T], metrics queueMetrics[T], updatePeriod time.Duration) *Typed[T] {
   164→	t := &Typed[T]{
   165→		clock:                      c,
   166→		queue:                      queue,
   167→		dirty:                      sets.Set[T]{},
   168→		processing:                 sets.Set[T]{},
   169→		cond:                       sync.NewCond(&sync.Mutex{}),
   170→		metrics:                    metrics,
   171→		unfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod: updatePeriod,
   172→		stopCh:                     make(chan struct{}),
   173→	}
   174→
   175→	// Don't start the goroutine for a type of noMetrics so we don't consume
   176→	// resources unnecessarily
   177→	if _, ok := metrics.(noMetrics[T]); !ok {
   178→		t.wg.Go(t.updateUnfinishedWorkLoop)
   179→	}
   180→
   181→	return t
   182→}
   183→
   184→const defaultUnfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod = 500 * time.Millisecond
   185→
   186→// Type is a work queue (see the package comment).
   187→// Deprecated: Use Typed instead.
   188→type Type = Typed[any]
   189→
   190→type Typed[t comparable] struct {
   191→	// queue defines the order in which we will work on items. Every
   192→	// element of queue should be in the dirty set and not in the
   193→	// processing set.
   194→	queue Queue[t]
   195→
   196→	// dirty defines all of the items that need to be processed.
   197→	dirty sets.Set[t]
   198→
   199→	// Things that are currently being processed are in the processing set.
   200→	// These things may be simultaneously in the dirty set. When we finish
   201→	// processing something and remove it from this set, we'll check if
   202→	// it's in the dirty set, and if so, add it to the queue.
   203→	processing sets.Set[t]
   204→
   205→	cond *sync.Cond
   206→
   207→	shuttingDown bool
   208→	drain        bool
   209→
   210→	metrics queueMetrics[t]
   211→
   212→	unfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod time.Duration
   213→	clock                      clock.WithTicker
   214→
   215→	// wg manages goroutines started by the queue to allow graceful shutdown
   216→	// ShutDown() will wait for goroutines to exit before returning.
   217→	wg sync.WaitGroup
   218→
   219→	stopCh chan struct{}
   220→	// stopOnce guarantees we only signal shutdown a single time
   221→	stopOnce sync.Once
   222→}
   223→
   224→// Add marks item as needing processing. When the queue is shutdown new
   225→// items will silently be ignored and not queued or marked as dirty for
   226→// reprocessing.
   227→func (q *Typed[T]) Add(item T) {
   228→	q.cond.L.Lock()
   229→	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
   230→	if q.shuttingDown {
   231→		return
   232→	}
   233→	if q.dirty.Has(item) {
   234→		// the same item is added again before it is processed, call the Touch
   235→		// function if the queue cares about it (for e.g, reset its priority)
   236→		if !q.processing.Has(item) {
   237→			q.queue.Touch(item)
   238→		}
   239→		return
   240→	}
   241→
   242→	q.metrics.add(item)
   243→
   244→	q.dirty.Insert(item)
   245→	if q.processing.Has(item) {
   246→		return
   247→	}
   248→
   249→	q.queue.Push(item)
   250→	q.cond.Signal()
   251→}
   252→
   253→// Len returns the current queue length, for informational purposes only. You
   254→// shouldn't e.g. gate a call to Add() or Get() on Len() being a particular
   255→// value, that can't be synchronized properly.
   256→func (q *Typed[T]) Len() int {
   257→	q.cond.L.Lock()
   258→	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
   259→	return q.queue.Len()
   260→}
   261→
   262→// Get blocks until it can return an item to be processed. If shutdown = true,
   263→// the caller should end their goroutine. You must call Done with item when you
   264→// have finished processing it.
   265→func (q *Typed[T]) Get() (item T, shutdown bool) {
   266→	q.cond.L.Lock()
   267→	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
   268→	for q.queue.Len() == 0 && !q.shuttingDown {
   269→		q.cond.Wait()
   270→	}
   271→	if q.queue.Len() == 0 {
   272→		// We must be shutting down.
   273→		return *new(T), true
   274→	}
   275→
   276→	item = q.queue.Pop()
   277→
   278→	q.metrics.get(item)
   279→
   280→	q.processing.Insert(item)
   281→	q.dirty.Delete(item)
   282→
   283→	return item, false
   284→}
   285→
   286→// Done marks item as done processing, and if it has been marked as dirty again
   287→// while it was being processed, it will be re-added to the queue for
   288→// re-processing.
   289→func (q *Typed[T]) Done(item T) {
   290→	q.cond.L.Lock()
   291→	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
   292→
   293→	q.metrics.done(item)
   294→
   295→	q.processing.Delete(item)
   296→	if q.dirty.Has(item) {
   297→		q.queue.Push(item)
   298→		q.cond.Signal()
   299→	} else if q.processing.Len() == 0 {
   300→		q.cond.Signal()
   301→	}
   302→}
   303→
   304→// ShutDown will cause q to ignore all new items added to it. Worker
   305→// goroutines will continue processing items in the queue until it is
   306→// empty and then receive the shutdown signal.
   307→func (q *Typed[T]) ShutDown() {
   308→	defer q.wg.Wait()
   309→	q.stopOnce.Do(func() {
   310→		defer close(q.stopCh)
   311→	})
   312→
   313→	q.cond.L.Lock()
   314→	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
   315→
   316→	q.drain = false
   317→	q.shuttingDown = true
   318→	q.cond.Broadcast()
   319→}
   320→
   321→// ShutDownWithDrain is equivalent to ShutDown but waits until all items
   322→// in the queue have been processed.
   323→// ShutDown can be called after ShutDownWithDrain to force
   324→// ShutDownWithDrain to stop waiting.
   325→// Workers must call Done on an item after processing it, otherwise
   326→// ShutDownWithDrain will block indefinitely.
   327→func (q *Typed[T]) ShutDownWithDrain() {
   328→	defer q.wg.Wait()
   329→	q.stopOnce.Do(func() {
   330→		defer close(q.stopCh)
   331→	})
   332→	q.cond.L.Lock()
   333→	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
   334→
   335→	q.drain = true
   336→	q.shuttingDown = true
   337→	q.cond.Broadcast()
   338→
   339→	for q.processing.Len() != 0 && q.drain {
   340→		q.cond.Wait()
   341→	}
   342→}
   343→
   344→func (q *Typed[T]) ShuttingDown() bool {
   345→	q.cond.L.Lock()
   346→	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
   347→
   348→	return q.shuttingDown
   349→}
   350→
   351→func (q *Typed[T]) updateUnfinishedWork() {
   352→	q.cond.L.Lock()
   353→	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
   354→	if !q.shuttingDown {
   355→		q.metrics.updateUnfinishedWork()
   356→	}
   357→}
   358→
   359→func (q *Typed[T]) updateUnfinishedWorkLoop() {
   360→	t := q.clock.NewTicker(q.unfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod)
   361→	defer t.Stop()
   362→	for {
   363→		select {
   364→		case <-t.C():
   365→			q.updateUnfinishedWork()
   366→		case <-q.stopCh:
   367→			return
   368→		}
   369→	}
   370→}

It is optional and defaults to slice based FIFO queue.\n\tQueue Queue[T]\n}\n\n// New constructs a new work queue (see the package comment).\n//\n// Deprecated: use NewTyped instead.\nfunc New() *Type {\n\treturn NewWithConfig(QueueConfig{\n\t\tName: \"\",\n\t})\n}\n\n// NewTyped constructs a new work queue (see the package comment).\nfunc NewTyped[T comparable]() *Typed[T] {\n\treturn different properties.\nfunc NewTypedWithConfig[T comparable](config TypedQueueConfig[T]) *Typed[T] {\n\treturn newQueueWithConfig(config, defaultUnfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod)\n}\n\n// NewNamed creates a new named queue.\n// Deprecated: Use NewWithConfig instead.\nfunc NewNamed(name string) *Type {\n\treturn NewWithConfig(QueueConfig{\n\t\tName: name,\n\t})\n}\n\n// newQueueWithConfig constructs a new named workqueue\n// with the ability to customize different properties for testing purposes\nfunc newQueueWithConfig[T comparable](config TypedQueueConfig[T], updatePeriod time.Duration) *Typed[T] {\n\tmetricsProvider := globalMetricsProvider\n\tif config.MetricsProvider != nil {\n\t\tmetricsProvider = config.MetricsProvider\n\t}\n\n\tif config.Clock == nil {\n\t\tconfig.Clock = clock.RealClock{}\n\t}\n\n\tif config.Queue == nil {\n\t\tconfig.Queue = DefaultQueue[T]()\n\t}\n\n\treturn newQueue(\n\t\tconfig.Clock,\n\t\tconfig.Queue,\n\t\tnewQueueMetrics[T](metricsProvider, config.Name, config.Clock),\n\t\tupdatePeriod,\n\t)\n}\n\nfunc newQueue[T comparable](c clock.WithTicker, queue Queue[T], metrics queueMetrics[T], updatePeriod time.Duration) *Typed[T] {\n\tt := &Typed[T]{\n\t\tclock:                      c,\n\t\tqueue:                      queue,\n\t\tdirty:                      sets.Set[T]{},\n\t\tprocessing:                 sets.Set[T]{},\n\t\tcond:                       sync.NewCond(&sync.Mutex{}),\n\t\tmetrics:                    metrics,\n\t\tunfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod: updatePeriod,\n\t\tstopCh:                     make(chan struct{}),\n\t}\n\n\t// Don't start the goroutine for a type of noMetrics so we don't consume\n\t// resources unnecessarily\n\tif _, ok := metrics.(noMetrics[T]); !ok {\n\t\tt.wg.Go(t.updateUnfinishedWorkLoop)\n\t}\n\n\treturn t\n}\n\nconst defaultUnfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod = 500 * time.Millisecond\n\n// Type is a work queue (see the package comment).\n// Deprecated: Use Typed instead.\ntype Type = Typed[any]\n\ntype Typed[t comparable] struct {\n\t// queue defines the order in which we will work on items. Every\n\t// element of queue should be in the dirty set and not in the\n\t// processing set.\n\tqueue Queue[t]\n\n\t// dirty defines all of the items that need to be processed.\n\tdirty sets.Set[t]\n\n\t// Things that are currently being processed are in the processing set.\n\t// These things may be simultaneously in the dirty set. When we finish\n\t// processing something and remove it from this set, we'll check if\n\t// it's in the dirty set, and if so, add it to the queue.\n\tprocessing sets.Set[t]\n\n\tcond *sync.Cond\n\n\tshuttingDown bool\n\tdrain        bool\n\n\tmetrics queueMetrics[t]\n\n\tunfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod time.Duration\n\tclock                      clock.WithTicker\n\n\t// wg manages goroutines started by the queue to allow graceful shutdown\n\t// ShutDown() will wait for goroutines to exit before returning.\n\twg sync.WaitGroup\n\n\tstopCh chan struct{}\n\t// stopOnce guarantees we only signal shutdown a single time\n\tstopOnce sync.Once\n}\n\n// Add marks item as needing processing. When the queue is shutdown new\n// items will silently be ignored and not queued or marked as dirty for\n// reprocessing.\nfunc (q *Typed[T]) Add(item T) {\n\tq.cond.L.Lock()\n\tdefer q.cond.L.Unlock()\n\tif q.shuttingDown {\n\t\treturn\n\t}\n\tif q.dirty.Has(item) {\n\t\t// the same item is added again before it is processed, call the Touch\n\t\t// function if the queue cares about it (for e.g, reset its priority)\n\t\tif !q.processing.Has(item) {\n\t\t\tq.queue.Touch(item)\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn\n\t}\n\n\tq.metrics.add(item)\n\n\tq.dirty.Insert(item)\n\tif q.processing.Has(item) {\n\t\treturn\n\t}\n\n\tq.queue.Push(item)\n\tq.cond.Signal()\n}\n\n// Len returns the current queue length, for informational purposes only.     60→	DelayingQueue TypedDelayingInterface[T]

    28→// Store is a generic object storage and processing interface.  A
    29→// Store holds a map from string keys to accumulators, and has
    30→// operations to add, update, and delete a given object to/from the
    31→// accumulator currently associated with a given key.  A Store also
    32→// knows how to extract the key from a given object, so many operations
    33→// are given only the object.
    34→//
    35→// In the simplest Store implementations each accumulator is simply
    36→// the last given object, or empty after Delete, and thus the Store's
    37→// behavior is simple storage.
    38→//
    39→// Reflector knows how to watch a server and update a Store.  This
    40→// package provides a variety of implementations of Store.
    41→type Store interface {
    42→
    43→	// Add adds the given object to the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    44→	Add(obj interface{}) error
    45→
    46→	// Update updates the given object in the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    47→	Update(obj interface{}) error
    48→
    49→	// Delete deletes the given object from the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    50→	Delete(obj interface{}) error
    51→
    52→	// List returns a list of all the currently non-empty accumulators
    53→	List() []interface{}
    54→
    55→	// ListKeys returns a list of all the keys currently associated with non-empty accumulators
    56→	ListKeys() []string
    57→
    58→	// Get returns the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    59→	Get(obj interface{}) (item interface{}, exists bool, err error)
    60→
    61→	// GetByKey returns the accumulator associated with the given key
    62→	GetByKey(key string) (item interface{}, exists bool, err error)
    63→
    64→	// Replace will delete the contents of the store, using instead the
    65→	// given list. Store takes ownership of the list, you should not reference
    66→	// it after calling this function.
    67→	Replace([]interface{}, string) error
    68→
    69→	// Resync is meaningless in the terms appearing here but has
    70→	// meaning in some implementations that have non-trivial
    71→	// additional behavior (e.g., DeltaFIFO).
    72→	Resync() error
    73→}
    74→
    75→// TransactionType defines the type of a transaction operation. It is used to indicate whether
    76→// an object is being added, updated, or deleted.
    77→type TransactionType string
    78→
    79→const (
    80→	TransactionTypeAdd    TransactionType = "Add"
    81→	TransactionTypeUpdate TransactionType = "Update"
    82→	TransactionTypeDelete TransactionType = "Delete"
    83→)
    84→
    85→// Transaction represents a single operation or event in a process. It holds a generic Object
    86→// associated with the transaction and a Type indicating the kind of transaction being performed.
    87→type Transaction struct {
    88→	Object interface{}
    89→	Type   TransactionType
    90→}
    91→
    92→type TransactionStore interface {
    93→	// Transaction allows multiple operations to occur within a single lock acquisition to
    94→	// ensure progress can be made when there is contention.
    95→	Transaction(txns ...Transaction) *TransactionError
    96→}

This\n// package provides a variety of implementations of Store.\ntype Store interface {\n\n\t// Add adds the given object to the accumulator associated with the given object's key\n\tAdd(obj interface{}) error\n\n\t// Update updates the given object in the accumulator associated with the given object's key\n\tUpdate(obj interface{}) error\n\n\t// Delete deletes the given object from the accumulator associated with the given object's key\n\tDelete(obj interface{}) error\n\n\t// List returns a list of all the currently non-empty accumulators\n\tList() []interface{}\n\n\t// ListKeys returns a list of all the keys currently associated with non-empty accumulators\n\tListKeys() []string\n\n\t// Get returns the accumulator associated with the given object's key\n\tGet(obj interface{}) (item interface{}, exists bool, err error)\n\n\t// GetByKey returns the accumulator associated with the given key\n\tGetByKey(key string) (item interface{}, exists bool, err error)\n\n\t// Replace will delete the contents of the store, using instead the\n\t// given list. Store takes ownership of the list, you should not reference\n\t// it after calling this function.\n\tReplace([]interface{}, string) error\n\n\t// Resync is meaningless in the terms appearing here but has\n\t// meaning in some implementations that have non-trivial\n\t// additional behavior (e.g., DeltaFIFO).\n\tResync() error\n}\n\n// TransactionType defines the type of a transaction operation. It is used to indicate whether\n// an object is being added, updated, or deleted.\ntype TransactionType string\n\nconst (\n\tTransactionTypeAdd    TransactionType = \"Add\"\n\tTransactionTypeUpdate TransactionType = \"Update\"\n\tTransactionTypeDelete TransactionType = \"Delete\"\n)\n\n// Transaction represents a single operation or event in a process. It holds a generic Object\n// associated with the transaction and a Type indicating the kind of transaction being performed.\ntype Transaction struct {\n\tObject interface{}\n\tType   TransactionType\n}\n\ntype TransactionStore interface {\n\t// Transaction allows multiple operations to occur within a single lock acquisition to\n\t// ensure progress can be made when there is contention.\n\tTransaction(txns ...Transaction) *TransactionError\n}\n\nvar _ error = &TransactionError{}\n\ntype TransactionError struct {", "numLines": 100, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 400}}

    17→package cache
    18→
    19→import (
    20→	"fmt"
    21→	"sync"
    22→	"time"
    23→
    24→	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/sets"
    25→	utiltrace "k8s.io/utils/trace"
    26→)
    27→
    28→// ThreadSafeStore is an interface that allows concurrent indexed
    29→// access to a storage backend.  It is like Indexer but does not
    30→// (necessarily) know how to extract the Store key from a given
    31→// object.
    32→//
    33→// TL;DR caveats: you must not modify anything returned by Get or List as it will break
    34→// the indexing feature in addition to not being thread safe.
    35→//
    36→// The guarantees of thread safety provided by List/Get are only valid if the caller
    37→// treats returned items as read-only. For example, a pointer inserted in the store
    38→// through `Add` will be returned as is by `Get`. Multiple clients might invoke `Get`
    39→// on the same key and modify the pointer in a non-thread-safe way. Also note that
    40→// modifying objects stored by the indexers (if any) will *not* automatically lead
    41→// to a re-index. So it's not a good idea to directly modify the objects returned by
    42→// Get/List, in general.
    43→type ThreadSafeStore interface {
    44→	Add(key string, obj interface{})
    45→	Update(key string, obj interface{})
    46→	Delete(key string)
    47→	Get(key string) (item interface{}, exists bool)
    48→	List() []interface{}
    49→	ListKeys() []string
    50→	Replace(map[string]interface{}, string)
    51→	Index(indexName string, obj interface{}) ([]interface{}, error)
    52→	IndexKeys(indexName, indexedValue string) ([]string, error)
    53→	ListIndexFuncValues(name string) []string
    54→	ByIndex(indexName, indexedValue string) ([]interface{}, error)
    55→	GetIndexers() Indexers
    56→
    57→	// AddIndexers adds more indexers to this store. This supports adding indexes after the store already has items.
    58→	AddIndexers(newIndexers Indexers) error
    59→	// Resync is a no-op and is deprecated
    60→	Resync() error
    61→}
    62→
    63→// ThreadSafeStoreWithTransaction is a store that can batch execute multiple transactions.
    64→type ThreadSafeStoreWithTransaction interface {
    65→	ThreadSafeStore
    66→	// Transaction allows performing multiple writes in one call.
    67→	Transaction(fns ...ThreadSafeStoreTransaction)
    68→}
    69→
    70→// ThreadSafeStoreTransaction embeds a Transaction and includes the specific Key identifying the affected object.
    71→type ThreadSafeStoreTransaction struct {
    72→	Transaction
    73→	Key string
    74→}
    75→
    76→// storeIndex implements the indexing functionality for Store interface
    77→type storeIndex struct {
    78→	// indexers maps a name to an IndexFunc
    79→	indexers Indexers
    80→	// indices maps a name to an Index
    81→	indices Indices
    82→}
    83→
    84→func (i *storeIndex) reset() {
    85→	i.indices = Indices{}
    86→}
    87→
    88→func (i *storeIndex) getKeysFromIndex(indexName string, obj interface{}) (sets.Set[string], error) {
    89→	indexFunc := i.indexers[indexName]
    90→	if indexFunc == nil {
    91→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("Index with name %s does not exist", indexName)
    92→	}
    93→
    94→	indexedValues, err := indexFunc(obj)
    95→	if err != nil {
    96→		return nil, err
    97→	}
    98→	index := i.indices[indexName]
    99→
   100→	var storeKeySet sets.Set[string]

    17→// Package leaderelection implements leader election of a set of endpoints.
    18→// It uses an annotation in the endpoints object to store the record of the
    19→// election state. This implementation does not guarantee that only one
    20→// client is acting as a leader (a.k.a. fencing).
    21→//
    22→// A client only acts on timestamps captured locally to infer the state of the
    23→// leader election. The client does not consider timestamps in the leader
    24→// election record to be accurate because these timestamps may not have been
    25→// produced by a local clock. The implemention does not depend on their
    26→// accuracy and only uses their change to indicate that another client has
    27→// renewed the leader lease. Thus the implementation is tolerant to arbitrary
    28→// clock skew, but is not tolerant to arbitrary clock skew rate.
    29→//
    30→// However the level of tolerance to skew rate can be configured by setting
    31→// RenewDeadline and LeaseDuration appropriately. The tolerance expressed as a
    32→// maximum tolerated ratio of time passed on the fastest node to time passed on
    33→// the slowest node can be approximately achieved with a configuration that sets
    34→// the same ratio of LeaseDuration to RenewDeadline. For example if a user wanted
    35→// to tolerate some nodes progressing forward in time twice as fast as other nodes,
    36→// the user could set LeaseDuration to 60 seconds and RenewDeadline to 30 seconds.
    37→//
    38→// While not required, some method of clock synchronization between nodes in the
    39→// cluster is highly recommended. It's important to keep in mind when configuring
    40→// this client that the tolerance to skew rate varies inversely to master
    41→// availability.
    42→//
    43→// Larger clusters often have a more lenient SLA for API latency. This should be
    44→// taken into account when configuring the client. The rate of leader transitions
    45→// should be monitored and RetryPeriod and LeaseDuration should be increased
    46→// until the rate is stable and acceptably low. It's important to keep in mind
    47→// when configuring this client that the tolerance to API latency varies inversely
    48→// to master availability.
    49→//
    50→// DISCLAIMER: this is an alpha API. This library will likely change significantly
    51→// or even be removed entirely in subsequent releases. Depend on this API at
    52→// your own risk.
    53→package leaderelection

    56→	"bytes"

    65→	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/wait"

    75→// NewLeaderElector creates a LeaderElector from a LeaderElectionConfig
    76→func NewLeaderElector(lec LeaderElectionConfig) (*LeaderElector, error) {
    77→	if lec.LeaseDuration <= lec.RenewDeadline {

    79→	}

    81→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("renewDeadline must be greater than retryPeriod*JitterFactor")
    82→	}

    85→	}

    96→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("OnStoppedLeading callback must not be nil")

    30→// Lister is any object that knows how to perform an initial list.
    31→//
    32→// Ideally, all implementations of Lister should also implement ListerWithContext.
    33→type Lister interface {
    34→	// List should return a list type object; the Items field will be extracted, and the
    35→	// ResourceVersion field will be used to start the watch in the right place.
    36→	//
    37→	// Deprecated: use ListerWithContext.ListWithContext instead.
    38→	List(options metav1.ListOptions) (runtime.Object, error)
    39→}
    40→
    41→// ListerWithContext is any object that knows how to perform an initial list.
    42→type ListerWithContext interface {
    43→	// ListWithContext should return a list type object; the Items field will be extracted, and the
    44→	// ResourceVersion field will be used to start the watch in the right place.
    45→	ListWithContext(ctx context.Context, options metav1.ListOptions) (runtime.Object, error)
    46→}
    47→
    48→func ToListerWithContext(l Lister) ListerWithContext {
    49→	if l, ok := l.(ListerWithContext); ok {
    50→		return l
    51→	}
    52→	return listerWrapper{
    53→		parent: l,
    54→	}
    55→}
    56→
    57→type listerWrapper struct {
    58→	parent Lister
    59→}
    60→
    61→func (l listerWrapper) ListWithContext(ctx context.Context, options metav1.ListOptions) (runtime.Object, error) {
    62→	return l.parent.List(options)
    63→}
    64→
    65→// Watcher is any object that knows how to start a watch on a resource.
    66→//
    67→// Ideally, all implementations of Watcher should also implement WatcherWithContext.
    68→type Watcher interface {
    69→	// Watch should begin a watch at the specified version.
    70→	//
    71→	// If Watch returns an error, it should handle its own cleanup, including
    72→	// but not limited to calling Stop() on the watch, if one was constructed.
    73→	// This allows the caller to ignore the watch, if the error is non-nil.
    74→	//
    75→	// Deprecated: use WatcherWithContext.WatchWithContext instead.
    76→	Watch(options metav1.ListOptions) (watch.Interface, error)
    77→}
    78→
    79→// WatcherWithContext is any object that knows how to start a watch on a resource.

    85→	// This allows the caller to ignore the watch, if the error is non-nil.
    86→	WatchWithContext(ctx context.Context, options metav1.ListOptions) (watch.Interface, error)
    87→}
    88→
    89→func ToWatcherWithContext(w Watcher) WatcherWithContext {
    90→	if w, ok := w.(WatcherWithContext); ok {
    91→		return w
    92→	}
    93→	return watcherWrapper{

    95→	}

   407→// `*sharedIndexInformer` implements SharedIndexInformer and has three
   408→// main components.  One is an indexed local cache, `indexer Indexer`.
   409→// The second main component is a Controller that pulls
   410→// objects/notifications using the ListerWatcher and pushes them into
   411→// a DeltaFIFO --- whose knownObjects is the informer's local cache
   412→// --- while concurrently Popping Deltas values from that fifo and
   413→// processing them with `sharedIndexInformer::HandleDeltas`.  Each
   414→// invocation of HandleDeltas, which is done with the fifo's lock
   415→// held, processes each Delta in turn.  For each Delta this both
   416→// updates the local cache and stuffs the relevant notification into
   417→// the sharedProcessor.  The third main component is that
   418→// sharedProcessor, which is responsible for relaying those
   419→// notifications to each of the informer's clients.
   420→type sharedIndexInformer struct {
   421→	indexer    Indexer
   422→	controller Controller
   423→
   424→	processor             *sharedProcessor
   425→	cacheMutationDetector MutationDetector
   426→
   427→	listerWatcher ListerWatcher
   428→
   429→	// objectType is an example object of the type this informer is expected to handle. If set, an event
   430→	// with an object with a mismatching type is dropped instead of being delivered to listeners.
   431→	objectType runtime.Object
   432→
   433→	// objectDescription is the description of this informer's objects. This typically defaults to
   434→	objectDescription string
   435→
   436→	// resyncCheckPeriod is how often we want the reflector's resync timer to fire so it can call
   437→	// shouldResync to check if any of our listeners need a resync.
   438→	resyncCheckPeriod time.Duration
   439→	// defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod is the default resync period for any handlers added via
   440→	// AddEventHandler (i.e. they don't specify one and just want to use the shared informer's default
   441→	// value).
   442→	defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod time.Duration
   443→	// clock allows for testability
   444→	clock clock.Clock
   445→
   446→	started, stopped bool
   447→	startedLock      sync.Mutex
   448→
   449→	// blockDeltas gives a way to stop all event distribution so that a late event handler
   450→	// can safely join the shared informer.
   451→	blockDeltas sync.Mutex
   452→
   453→	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
   454→	watchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
   455→
   456→	transform TransformFunc
   457→
   458→	// keyFunc is called when processing deltas by the underlying process function.
   459→	keyFunc KeyFunc
   460→}
   461→
   462→// dummyController hides the fact that a SharedInformer is different from a dedicated one
   463→// where a caller can `Run`.  The run method is disconnected in this case, because higher
   464→// level logic will decide when to start the SharedInformer and related controller.
   465→// Because returning information back is always asynchronous, the legacy callers shouldn't
   466→// notice any change in behavior.
   467→type dummyController struct {
   468→	informer *sharedIndexInformer
   469→}
   470→
   471→func (v *dummyController) RunWithContext(context.Context) {
   472→}
   473→
   474→func (v *dummyController) Run(stopCh <-chan struct{}) {
   475→}
   476→
   477→func (v *dummyController) HasSynced() bool {
   478→	return v.informer.HasSynced()
   479→}
   480→
   481→func (v *dummyController) LastSyncResourceVersion() string {
   482→	if clientgofeaturegate.FeatureGates().Enabled(clientgofeaturegate.InformerResourceVersion) {
   483→		return v.informer.LastSyncResourceVersion()
   484→	}
   485→
   486→	return ""
   487→}
   488→
   489→type updateNotification struct {
   490→	oldObj interface{}
   491→	newObj interface{}
   492→}
   493→
   494→type addNotification struct {
   495→	newObj          interface{}
   496→	isInInitialList bool
   497→}
   498→
   499→type deleteNotification struct {
   500→	oldObj interface{}
   501→}
   502→
   503→func (s *sharedIndexInformer) SetWatchErrorHandler(handler WatchErrorHandler) error {
   504→	return s.SetWatchErrorHandlerWithContext(func(_ context.Context, r *Reflector, err error) {
   505→		handler(r, err)
   506→	})
   507→}
   508→
   509→func (s *sharedIndexInformer) SetWatchErrorHandlerWithContext(handler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext) error {
   510→	s.startedLock.Lock()
   511→	defer s.startedLock.Unlock()
   512→
   513→	if s.started {
   514→		return fmt.Errorf("informer has already started")
   515→	}
   516→
   517→	s.watchErrorHandler = handler
   518→	return nil
   519→}
   520→
   521→func (s *sharedIndexInformer) SetTransform(handler TransformFunc) error {
   522→	s.startedLock.Lock()
   523→	defer s.startedLock.Unlock()
   524→
   525→	if s.started {
   526→		return fmt.Errorf("informer has already started")
   527→	}
   528→
   529→	s.transform = handler
   530→	return nil
   531→}
   532→
   533→func (s *sharedIndexInformer) Run(stopCh <-chan struct{}) {
   534→	s.RunWithContext(wait.ContextForChannel(stopCh))
   535→}
   536→
   537→func (s *sharedIndexInformer) RunWithContext(ctx context.Context) {
   538→	defer utilruntime.HandleCrashWithContext(ctx)
   539→	logger := klog.FromContext(ctx)
   540→
   541→	if s.HasStarted() {
   542→		logger.Info("Warning: the sharedIndexInformer has started, run more than once is not allowed")
   543→		return
   544→	}
   545→
   546→	func() {
   547→		s.startedLock.Lock()
   548→		defer s.startedLock.Unlock()
   549→
   550→		fifo := newQueueFIFO(s.indexer, s.transform)
   551→
   552→		cfg := &Config{
   553→			Queue:             fifo,
   554→			ListerWatcher:     s.listerWatcher,
   555→			ObjectType:        s.objectType,
   556→			ObjectDescription: s.objectDescription,
   557→			FullResyncPeriod:  s.resyncCheckPeriod,
   558→			ShouldResync:      s.processor.shouldResync,
   559→
   560→			Process:                      s.HandleDeltas,
   561→			ProcessBatch:                 s.HandleBatchDeltas,
   562→			WatchErrorHandlerWithContext: s.watchErrorHandler,
   563→		}
   564→
   565→		s.controller = New(cfg)
   566→		s.controller.(*controller).clock = s.clock
   567→		s.started = true
   568→	}()
   569→
   570→	// Separate stop context because Processor should be stopped strictly after controller.
   571→	// Cancelation in the parent context is ignored and all values are passed on,
   572→	// including - but not limited to - a logger.
   573→	processorStopCtx, stopProcessor := context.WithCancelCause(context.WithoutCancel(ctx))
   574→	var wg wait.Group
   575→	defer wg.Wait()                                         // Wait for Processor to stop
   576→	defer stopProcessor(errors.New("informer is stopping")) // Tell Processor to stop
   577→	// TODO: extend the MutationDetector interface so that it optionally
   578→	// has a RunWithContext method that we can use here.
   579→	wg.StartWithChannel(processorStopCtx.Done(), s.cacheMutationDetector.Run)
   580→	wg.StartWithContext(processorStopCtx, s.processor.run)
   581→
   582→	defer func() {
   583→		s.startedLock.Lock()
   584→		defer s.startedLock.Unlock()
   585→		s.stopped = true // Don't want any new listeners
   586→	}()
   587→	s.controller.RunWithContext(ctx)
   588→}

   590→func (s *sharedIndexInformer) HasStarted() bool {

One is an indexed local cache, `indexer Indexer`.\n// The second main component is a Controller that pulls\n// objects/notifications using the ListerWatcher and pushes them into\n// a DeltaFIFO --- whose knownObjects is the informer's local cache\n// --- while concurrently Popping Deltas values from that fifo and\n// processing them with `sharedIndexInformer::HandleDeltas`.  Each\n// invocation of HandleDeltas, which is done with the fifo's lock\n// held, processes each Delta in turn.  For each Delta this both\n// updates the local cache and stuffs the relevant notification into\n// the sharedProcessor.  The third main component is that\n// sharedProcessor, which is responsible for relaying those\n// notifications to each of the informer's clients.\ntype sharedIndexInformer struct {\n\tindexer    Indexer\n\tcontroller Controller\n\n\tprocessor             *sharedProcessor\n\tcacheMutationDetector MutationDetector\n\n\tlisterWatcher ListerWatcher\n\n\t// objectType is an example object of the type this informer is expected to handle. If set, an event\n\t// with an object with a mismatching type is dropped instead of being delivered to listeners.\n\tobjectType runtime.Object\n\n\t// objectDescription is the description of this informer's objects. This typically defaults to\n\tobjectDescription string\n\n\t// resyncCheckPeriod is how often we want the reflector's resync timer to fire so it can call\n\t// shouldResync to check if any of our listeners need a resync.\n\tresyncCheckPeriod time.Duration\n\t// defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod is the default resync period for any handlers added via\n\t// AddEventHandler (i.e. they don't specify one and just want to use the shared informer's default\n\t// value).\n\tdefaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod time.Duration\n\t// clock allows for testability\n\tclock clock.Clock\n\n\tstarted, stopped bool\n\tstartedLock      sync.Mutex\n\n\t// blockDeltas gives a way to stop all event distribution so that a late event handler\n\t// can safely join the shared informer.\n\tblockDeltas sync.Mutex\n\n\t// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.\n\twatchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext\n\n\ttransform TransformFunc\n\n\t// keyFunc is called when processing deltas by the underlying process function.\n\tkeyFunc KeyFunc\n}\n\n// dummyController hides the fact that a SharedInformer is different from a dedicated one\n// where a caller can `Run`.  The run method is disconnected in this case, because higher\n// level logic will decide when to start the SharedInformer and related controller.\n// Because returning information back is always asynchronous, the legacy callers shouldn't\n// notice any change in behavior.\ntype dummyController struct {\n\tinformer *sharedIndexInformer\n}\n\nfunc (v *dummyController) RunWithContext(context.Context) {\n}\n\nfunc (v *dummyController) Run(stopCh <-chan struct{}) {\n}\n\nfunc (v *dummyController) HasSynced() bool {\n\treturn v.informer.HasSynced()\n}\n\nfunc (v *dummyController) LastSyncResourceVersion() string {\n\tif clientgofeaturegate.FeatureGates().Enabled(clientgofeaturegate.InformerResourceVersion) {\n\t\treturn v.informer.LastSyncResourceVersion()\n\t}\n\n\treturn \"\"\n}\n\ntype updateNotification struct {\n\toldObj interface{}\n\tnewObj interface{}\n}\n\ntype addNotification struct {\n\tnewObj          interface{}\n\tisInInitialList bool\n}\n\ntype deleteNotification struct {\n\toldObj interface{}\n}\n\nfunc (s *sharedIndexInformer) SetWatchErrorHandler(handler WatchErrorHandler) error {\n\treturn = handler\n\treturn nil\n}\n\nfunc (s *sharedIndexInformer) SetTransform(handler TransformFunc) error {\n\ts.startedLock.Lock()\n\tdefer s.startedLock.Unlock()\n\n\tif s.started {\n\t\treturn     17→package features
    18→
    19→import (
    20→	"errors"
    21→	"sync/atomic"
    22→
    23→	utilruntime "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime"
    24→	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/version"
    25→)
    26→
    27→// NOTE: types Feature, FeatureSpec, prerelease (and its values)
    28→// were duplicated from the component-base repository
    29→//
    30→// for more information please refer to https://docs.google.com/document/d/1g9BGCRw-7ucUxO6OtCWbb3lfzUGA_uU9178wLdXAIfs
    31→
    32→const (
    33→	// Values for PreRelease.
    34→	Alpha = prerelease("ALPHA")
    35→	Beta  = prerelease("BETA")
    36→	GA    = prerelease("")
    37→
    38→	// Deprecated
    39→	Deprecated = prerelease("DEPRECATED")
    40→)
    41→
    42→type prerelease string
    43→
    44→type Feature string
    45→
    46→type FeatureSpec struct {
    47→	// Default is the default enablement state for the feature
    48→	Default bool
    49→	// LockToDefault indicates that the feature is locked to its default and cannot be changed
    50→	LockToDefault bool
    51→	// PreRelease indicates the maturity level of the feature
    52→	PreRelease prerelease
    53→	// Version indicates the earliest version from which this FeatureSpec is valid.
    54→	// If multiple FeatureSpecs exist for a Feature, the one with the highest version that is less
    55→	// than or equal to the effective version of the component is used.
    56→	Version *version.Version
    57→}
    58→
    59→type VersionedSpecs []FeatureSpec
    60→
    61→// Gates indicates whether a given feature is enabled or not.
    62→type Gates interface {
    63→	// Enabled returns true if the key is enabled.
    64→	Enabled(key Feature) bool
    65→}
    66→
    67→// Registry represents an external feature gates registry.
    68→type Registry interface {
    69→	// Add adds existing feature gates to the provided registry.
    70→	//
    71→	// As of today, this method is used by AddFeaturesToExistingFeatureGates and
    72→	// ReplaceFeatureGates to take control of the features exposed by this library.
    73→	Add(map[Feature]FeatureSpec) error
    74→}
    75→
    76→// VersionedRegistry represents an external versioned feature gates registry.
    77→type VersionedRegistry interface {
    78→	// AddVersioned adds existing versioned feature gates to the provided registry.
    79→	//
    80→	// As of today, this method is used by AddVersionedFeaturesToExistingFeatureGates and
    81→	// ReplaceFeatureGates to take control of the features exposed by this library.
    82→	AddVersioned(in map[Feature]VersionedSpecs) error
    83→}
    84→
    85→// FeatureGates returns the feature gates exposed by this library.
    86→//
    87→// By default, only the default features gates will be returned.
    88→// The default implementation allows controlling the features
    89→// via environmental variables.
    90→// For example, if you have a feature named "MyFeature"
    91→// setting an environmental variable "KUBE_FEATURE_MyFeature"
    92→// will allow you to configure the state of that feature.
    93→//
    94→// Please note that the actual set of the feature gates
    95→// might be overwritten by calling ReplaceFeatureGates method.
    96→func FeatureGates() Gates {
    97→	return featureGates.Load().(*featureGatesWrapper).Gates
    98→}
    99→
   100→// AddFeaturesToExistingFeatureGates adds the default feature gates to the provided registry.
   101→// Usually this function is combined with ReplaceFeatureGates to take control of the
   102→// features exposed by this library.
   103→func AddFeaturesToExistingFeatureGates(registry Registry) error {
   104→	return registry.Add(unversionedFeatureGates(defaultVersionedKubernetesFeatureGates))
   105→}
   106→
   107→// AddFeaturesToExistingFeatureGates adds the default versioned feature gates to the provided registry.
   108→// Usually this function is combined with ReplaceFeatureGates to take control of the
   109→// features exposed by this library.
   110→// Generally only used by k/k.
   111→func AddVersionedFeaturesToExistingFeatureGates(registry VersionedRegistry) error {
   112→	return registry.AddVersioned(defaultVersionedKubernetesFeatureGates)
   113→}
   114→
   115→// ReplaceFeatureGates overwrites the default implementation of the feature gates
   116→// used by this library.
   117→//
   118→// Useful for binaries that would like to have full control of the features
   119→// exposed by this library, such as allowing consumers of a binary
   120→// to interact with the features via a command line flag.
   121→//
   122→// For example:
   123→//
   124→//	// first, register client-go's features to your registry.
   125→//	clientgofeaturegate.AddFeaturesToExistingFeatureGates(utilfeature.DefaultMutableFeatureGate)
   126→//	// then replace client-go's feature gates implementation with your implementation
   127→//	clientgofeaturegate.ReplaceFeatureGates(utilfeature.DefaultMutableFeatureGate)
   128→func ReplaceFeatureGates(newFeatureGates Gates) {
   129→	if replaceFeatureGatesWithWarningIndicator(newFeatureGates) {
   130→		utilruntime.HandleError(errors.New("the default feature gates implementation has already been used and now it's being overwritten. This might lead to unexpected behaviour. Check your initialization order"))
   131→	}
   132→}
   133→
   134→func replaceFeatureGatesWithWarningIndicator(newFeatureGates Gates) bool {
   135→	shouldProduceWarning := false
   136→
   137→	if defaultFeatureGates, ok := FeatureGates().(*envVarFeatureGates); ok {
   138→		if defaultFeatureGates.hasAlreadyReadEnvVar() {
   139→			shouldProduceWarning = true
   140→		}
   141→	}
   142→	wrappedFeatureGates := &featureGatesWrapper{newFeatureGates}
   143→	featureGates.Store(wrappedFeatureGates)
   144→
   145→	return shouldProduceWarning
   146→}
   147→
   148→// unversionedFeatureGates takes the latest entry from the VersionedSpecs of each feature, and clears out the version information,
   149→// so that the result can be used with an unversioned feature gate.
   150→func unversionedFeatureGates(featureGates map[Feature]VersionedSpecs) map[Feature]FeatureSpec {
   151→	unversioned := map[Feature]FeatureSpec{}
   152→	for feature, specs := range featureGates {
   153→		if len(specs) == 0 {
   154→			continue
   155→		}
   156→		latestSpec := specs[len(specs)-1]
   157→		latestSpec.Version = nil // Clear version information.
   158→		unversioned[feature] = latestSpec
   159→	}
   160→	return unversioned
   161→}
   162→
   163→func init() {
   164→	envVarGates := newEnvVarFeatureGates(unversionedFeatureGates(defaultVersionedKubernetesFeatureGates))
   165→
   166→	wrappedFeatureGates := &featureGatesWrapper{envVarGates}
   167→	featureGates.Store(wrappedFeatureGates)
   168→}
   169→
   170→// featureGatesWrapper a thin wrapper to satisfy featureGates variable (atomic.Value).
   171→// That is, all calls to Store for a given Value must use values of the same concrete type.
   172→type featureGatesWrapper struct {
   173→	Gates
   174→}
   175→
   176→var (
   177→	// featureGates is a shared global FeatureGates.
   178→	//
   179→	// Top-level commands/options setup that needs to modify this feature gates
   180→	// should use AddFeaturesToExistingFeatureGates followed by ReplaceFeatureGates.
   181→	featureGates = &atomic.Value{}
   182→)

   830→func (p *sharedProcessor) addListener(listener *processorListener) ResourceEventHandlerRegistration {
   831→	p.listenersLock.Lock()
   832→	defer p.listenersLock.Unlock()
   833→
   834→	if p.listeners == nil {
   835→		p.listeners = make(map[*processorListener]bool)
   836→	}
   837→
   838→	p.listeners[listener] = true
   839→
   840→	if p.listenersStarted {
   841→		p.wg.Start(listener.run)
   842→		p.wg.Start(listener.pop)
   843→	}
   844→
   845→	return listener
   846→}
   847→
   848→func (p *sharedProcessor) removeListener(handle ResourceEventHandlerRegistration) error {
   849→	p.listenersLock.Lock()
   850→	defer p.listenersLock.Unlock()
   851→
   852→	listener, ok := handle.(*processorListener)
   853→	if !ok {
   854→		return fmt.Errorf("invalid key type %t", handle)
   855→	} else if p.listeners == nil {
   856→		// No listeners are registered, do nothing
   857→		return nil
   858→	} else if _, exists := p.listeners[listener]; !exists {
   859→		// Listener is not registered, just do nothing
   860→		return nil
   861→	}
   862→
   863→	delete(p.listeners, listener)
   864→
   865→	if p.listenersStarted {
   866→		close(listener.addCh)
   867→	}
   868→
   869→	return nil
   870→}
   871→
   872→func (p *sharedProcessor) distribute(obj interface{}, sync bool) {
   873→	p.listenersLock.RLock()
   874→	defer p.listenersLock.RUnlock()
   875→
   876→	// Before we start blocking on writes to the listeners' channels,
   877→	// ensure that they all have been started. If the processor stops,
   878→	// p.listeners gets cleared, in which case we also continue here
   879→	// and return without doing anything.
   880→	for !p.listenersStarted && len(p.listeners) > 0 {
   881→		p.listenersRCond.Wait()
   882→	}
   883→
   884→	for listener, isSyncing := range p.listeners {
   885→		switch {
   886→		case !sync:
   887→			// non-sync messages are delivered to every listener
   888→			listener.add(obj)
   889→		case isSyncing:
   890→			// sync messages are delivered to every syncing listener
   891→			listener.add(obj)
   892→		default:
   893→			// skipping a sync obj for a non-syncing listener
   894→		}
   895→	}
   896→}

   898→// sharedProcessorRunHook can be used inside tests to execute additional code
   899→// at the start of sharedProcessor.run.
   900→var sharedProcessorRunHook atomic.Pointer[func()]

   902→func (p *sharedProcessor) run(ctx context.Context) {
   903→	func() {
   904→		hook := sharedProcessorRunHook.Load()
   905→		if hook != nil {
   906→			(*hook)()
   907→		}
   908→		// Changing listenersStarted needs a write lock.
   909→		p.listenersLock.Lock()
   910→		defer p.listenersLock.Unlock()
   911→		for listener := range p.listeners {
   912→			p.wg.Start(listener.run)
   913→			p.wg.Start(listener.pop)
   914→		}
   915→		p.listenersStarted = true
   916→		p.listenersRCond.Signal()
   917→	}()
   918→	<-ctx.Done()
   919→
   920→	p.listenersLock.Lock()
   921→	defer p.listenersLock.Unlock()
   922→	for listener := range p.listeners {
   923→		close(listener.addCh) // Tell .pop() to stop. .pop() will tell .run() to stop
   924→	}
   925→
   926→	// Wipe out list of listeners since they are now closed
   927→	// (processorListener cannot be re-used)
   928→	p.listeners = nil
   929→
   930→	// Reset to false since no listeners are running
   931→	p.listenersStarted = false
   932→

   934→	p.listenersRCond.Signal()
   935→
   936→	p.wg.Wait() // Wait for all .pop() and .run() to stop
   937→}
   938→
   939→// shouldResync queries every listener to determine if any of them need a resync, based on each
   940→// listener's resyncPeriod.
   941→func (p *sharedProcessor) shouldResync() bool {
   942→	p.listenersLock.Lock()
   943→	defer p.listenersLock.Unlock()
   944→
   945→	resyncNeeded := false
   946→	now := p.clock.Now()
   947→	for listener := range p.listeners {
   948→		// need to loop through all the listeners to see if they need to resync so we can prepare any
   949→		// listeners that are going to be resyncing.
   950→		shouldResync := listener.shouldResync(now)
   951→		p.listeners[listener] = shouldResync
   952→
   953→		if shouldResync {
   954→			resyncNeeded = true
   955→			listener.determineNextResync(now)
   956→		}
   957→	}
   958→	return resyncNeeded
   959→}

   962→	p.listenersLock.RLock()
   963→	defer p.listenersLock.RUnlock()

   966→		resyncPeriod := determineResyncPeriod(

    30→// IsRetryableErrorFunc allows the client to provide its own function
    31→// that determines whether the specified err from the server is retryable.
    32→//
    33→// request: the original request sent to the server
    34→// err: the server sent this error to us
    35→//
    36→// The function returns true if the error is retryable and the request
    37→// can be retried, otherwise it returns false.
    38→// We have four mode of communications - 'Stream', 'Watch', 'Do' and 'DoRaw', this
    39→// function allows us to customize the retryability aspect of each.
    40→type IsRetryableErrorFunc func(request *http.Request, err error) bool
    41→
    42→func (r IsRetryableErrorFunc) IsErrorRetryable(request *http.Request, err error) bool {
    43→	return r(request, err)
    44→}
    45→
    46→var neverRetryError = IsRetryableErrorFunc(func(_ *http.Request, _ error) bool {
    47→	return false
    48→})
    49→
    50→// WithRetry allows the client to retry a request up to a certain number of times
    51→// Note that WithRetry is not safe for concurrent use by multiple
    52→// goroutines without additional locking or coordination.
    53→type WithRetry interface {
    54→	// IsNextRetry advances the retry counter appropriately
    55→	// and returns true if the request should be retried,
    56→	// otherwise it returns false, if:
    57→	//  - we have already reached the maximum retry threshold.
    58→	//  - the error does not fall into the retryable category.
    59→	//  - the server has not sent us a 429, or 5xx status code and the
    60→	//    'Retry-After' response header is not set with a value.
    61→	//  - we need to seek to the beginning of the request body before we
    62→	//    initiate the next retry, the function should log an error and
    63→	//    return false if it fails to do so.
    64→	//
    65→	// restReq: the associated rest.Request
    66→	// httpReq: the HTTP Request sent to the server
    67→	// resp: the response sent from the server, it is set if err is nil
    68→	// err: the server sent this error to us, if err is set then resp is nil.
    69→	// f: a IsRetryableErrorFunc function provided by the client that determines
    70→	//    if the err sent by the server is retryable.
    71→	IsNextRetry(ctx context.Context, restReq *Request, httpReq *http.Request, resp *http.Response, err error, f IsRetryableErrorFunc) bool
    72→
    73→	// Before should be invoked prior to each attempt, including
    74→	// the first one. If an error is returned, the request should
    75→	// be aborted immediately.
    76→	//
    77→	// Before may also be additionally responsible for preparing
    78→	// the request for the next retry, namely in terms of resetting
    79→	// the request body in case it has been read.
    80→	Before(ctx context.Context, r *Request) error

'Stream', 'Watch', 'Do' and 'DoRaw', this\n// function allows us to customize the retryability aspect of each.\ntype IsRetryableErrorFunc func(request *http.Request, err error) bool\n\nfunc (r IsRetryableErrorFunc) IsErrorRetryable(request *http.Request, err error) bool {\n\treturn r(request, err)\n}\n\nvar neverRetryError = IsRetryableErrorFunc(func(_ *http.Request, _ error) bool {\n\treturn false\n})\n\n// WithRetry allows the client to retry a request up to a certain number of times\n// Note that WithRetry is not safe for concurrent use by multiple\n// goroutines without additional locking or coordination.\ntype WithRetry interface {\n\t// IsNextRetry advances the retry counter appropriately\n\t// and returns true if the request should be retried,\n\t// otherwise it returns false, if:\n\t//  -
